description:
You are a network engineer who has just been paged because several production
    routers stopped sending flow-records to the collector.  
    Before opening a ticket with the storage team you want to rule-out any
    possibility that the server has simply run out of space (or is about to).

    1. From your *home directory* (/home/user) create a text file named  
       /home/user/network_disk_audit.log

    2. Populate that file with the following **exact** structure
       (the automated tests will look for the precise order, spelling and
       blank-lines shown below):

       ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
       Line-1  : === DISK USAGE REPORT ===
       Line-2  : Generated: <ISO-8601-timestamp>          ← e.g. 2024-05-01T11:42:07Z
       Line-3  :                                           ← must be a completely blank line
       Line-4  : [DF SUMMARY]
       Line-5+ : (verbatim output of the command “df -hT / /var”)  
                 • keep the command header that df prints (“Filesystem …”)  
                 • include every line that df emits for the requested paths  
       <blank line>
       [TOP20 /var/log]
       (verbatim output of the command  
        “du -ah /var/log | sort -hr | head -n 20”)  
                 • keep the sizes and paths exactly as the command prints  
                 • there **must** be exactly 20 data lines after the header
       ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

       Summary of required formatting rules:
       • Lines 1–4 must be exactly as shown (case-sensitive).  
       • There must be a single blank line **after** “Generated: …” and another
         single blank line **after** the df output block.  
       • The two bracketed headers (“[DF SUMMARY]” and “[TOP20 /var/log]”) must
         each appear on their own line.  
       • After “[TOP20 /var/log]” exactly 20 lines of data must follow.

    3. Save the file and leave it in place.  
       That’s it—no other files should be created or modified.

    Hints (do **not** reveal commands directly):
       • One command is helpful for showing mounted filesystems in human-readable
         format.
       • Another is useful for recursively summarising directory sizes.  
       • A sorting utility can sort by size, and a third utility can limit the
         number of lines kept.  
       • Remember you can redirect command output to files and use append mode.

    The automatic grader will confirm:
       • /home/user/network_disk_audit.log exists.  
       • The first four lines are exactly as specified.  
       • There is at least one line of df output and exactly 20 lines in the
         “TOP20” section.  
       • The headers and required blank lines are present in the correct places.
truth:
# Items the grader will check:

    file_expected_path: /home/user/network_disk_audit.log

    The file must satisfy all of these tests:

    1. First four lines (1-based):
       L1 "=== DISK USAGE REPORT ==="
       L2 starts with "Generated: "
       L3 ""              (completely blank)
       L4 "[DF SUMMARY]"

    2. Somewhere after line 4 must appear a line that starts with "Filesystem"
       (df’s header).  At least one additional line of df data must follow it.

    3. After the df data block there must be a single blank line, followed by
       the header line "[TOP20 /var/log]".

    4. Exactly 20 non-empty lines must follow that header (the du/sort/head
       output).

    5. No extra content appears beyond those 20 lines (i.e. total_lines =
       4 + df_header_and_rows + 1_blank + 1_header + 20_du_lines)

    The grader ignores the actual numerical sizes, filesystem names, and paths;
    it only checks structure and line counts.
